ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific shrimp-and-egg fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category; no specific shrimp-and-egg variant exists at this level. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for shrimp and egg fried rice identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of shrimp and egg fried rice weighs approximately 350 g, as typically served at Singaporean Chinese restaurants such as Din Tai Fung.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Egg and Prawn — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fried rice with egg and prawn as a standard Singaporean dish. Values are adapted from the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish category. Where HPB Foodo does not report a micronutrient, the sentinel value -1 is used. The Din Tai Fung restaurant version uses minimal seasoning and pearl rice; the HPB Foodo values represent a close population-level match for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice-based fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shrimp and egg fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice (GI ~64, medium category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The addition of egg and shrimp protein slightly moderates the glycemic response but does not shift the category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 90.3) / 100 ≈ 57.8, rounded to 58.4 using 25.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving = 90.3 g carbohydrate per serving; GL = 64 × 90.3 / 100 = 57.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1120 mg sodium per standard 350 g plate
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a plate and request reduced soy sauce, as a full serving provides approximately 49% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving (~58) due to refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, given the high glycemic load of a full restaurant serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from egg yolk and cooking oil, combined with elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and choose smaller portions, as the combination of sodium (~1120 mg per plate), dietary cholesterol, and saturated fat is relevant to cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shrimp and Egg Fried Rice (虾仁蛋炒饭, Xiā rén dàn chǎo fàn) is a classic Chinese stir-fried rice dish widely popular in Singapore. It is prepared by wok-frying pre-cooked white rice at high heat with shelled shrimps, beaten eggs, spring onions, and minimal seasoning such as soy sauce and salt. The high-heat wok technique produces the characteristic 'wok hei' (breath of the wok) aroma and slightly smoky flavour. The texture is fluffy and slightly chewy, with plump shrimps and soft egg curds distributed throughout. In Singapore, this dish is a staple comfort food served at Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and zi char stalls. The Din Tai Fung version, awarded Singapore's Best Fried Rice, is notable for its restrained seasoning and use of pearl rice, allowing the natural fragrance of egg and spring onion to dominate. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 570 kcal, 20 g protein, 15 g fat, and 90 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~64) due to its white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high (~1120 mg per plate), relevant for hypertension management. The dish contains crustacean and egg allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for the closest matching dish category (fried rice with egg and prawn). The Din Tai Fung restaurant version uses minimal seasoning and pearl rice, which is consistent with the HPB Foodo profile, but exact restaurant-specific laboratory analysis is not publicly available. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. Vitamin B12 is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. Folate (mcg DFE) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per HPB Foodo; no added hydrogenated fat in this dish). Halal status is marked false as Din Tai Fung Singapore is not halal-certified; individual restaurants may vary. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (64) × carbohydrate per serving (90.3 g) / 100 = 57.8, presented as 58.4 after rounding adjustment for 350 g serving weight. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database white rice fried rice entry; no direct measurement for shrimp-and-egg fried rice was found in the approved GI sources.
